data.title: Wall Street is mixed after PCE index data comes inline
data.description:
Wall Street is mixed on Friday after the Federal Reserve’s preferred measure of inflation gauge showed results in line with expectations, easing investor concerns over price pressures and bolstering market sentiment.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) has pushed up by 0.4%. At the same time,the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) has advanced 0.2%, and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) trades lower by 0.1%.

From a sector-by-sector stance, nine of the 11 S&P groups are in positive trading territory, with Energy leading the way. At the other end of the spectrum, Consumer Staples has suffered the most. 

U.S. Treasury yields remain mostly unchanged. The longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y)  is up 1 basis point to 4.18% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) is flat at 3.66%.

On the economic front, the Core Personal Consumption Expenditure Price Index (excludes food and energy) came in at +0.2% vs. +0.2% consensus and +0.2% prior (revised from +0.3%), and +2.9% Y/Y vs. +2.9% consensus and +2.9% in July. 

The PCE Price Index came in at +0.3% vs. +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior, and +2.7% Y/Y vs. +2.7% consensus and +2.6% prior. 

“Friday's PCE for August was as expected, and although it is still firmly above the Federal Reserve's 2% target, we believe the central bank remains on track to cut interest rates again at its next meeting in October, since it's clear that inflation is stable enough to handle lower interest rates,” Clark Bellin, president and chief investment officer of Bellwether Wealth stated. 

Furthermore, the September U.S. Consumer Sentiment Index also arrived. 

On tariff news, President Donald Trump announced sweeping new tariffs, including 100% on branded and patented pharmaceutical drugs, to take effect on October 1, with an exemption if the company is actively building a new manufacturing facility in the U.S. 

“The 100% pharmaceutical tariff applies only to branded drugs, and constructing a factory may lead to an exemption. Many pharmaceutical companies have facilities in the U.S., so it may be relatively easy to superficially expand those facilities to avoid tariffs being applied,” UBS' Paul Donovan said.

data.title: Ørsted in talks to sell 50% stake in flagship U.K. offshore wind project to Apollo - FT
data.description:
Ørsted (OTCPK:DNNGY) is in talks to sell a 50% stake in its £8.5 billion Hornsea 3 wind farm off the U.K. east coast to Apollo Global Management (APO), the Financial Times reported Friday, as the world's largest offshore wind developer tries to shore up its balance sheet.

Ørsted (OTCPK:DNNGY) launched a sale process for Hornsea 3 late last year and said in August that it had found a preferred bidder for the stake, without revealing the identity; the FT report now says the negotiations are with Apollo (APO).

A Hornsea 3 sale would bring an additional boost to Ørsted (OTCPK:DNNGY) in a week when a U.S. judge lifted a Trump administration order to stop work on its Revolution Wind project off the coast of Rhode Island.

Apollo (APO) has been an active dealmaker in European energy infrastructure, moving earlier this month with RWE to invest in Germany's electricity grid and $4.5 billion in financing announced in June for the U.K.'s Hinkley Point nuclear energy plant.

data.title: Private credit market set to exceed $30T – Blackstone
data.description:
The private credit market has the potential to grow to more than $30T, according to Dan Leiter, head of International at Blackstone (BX) Credit and Insurance.

This astronomical growth represents a massive expansion of what has traditionally been a much smaller segment of the financial markets.

In an interview with CNBC, Leiter emphasized that while private credit isn’t new, the recent acceleration in its growth has been extraordinary.

“The existing market, just for context, is about $2T in size,” Leiter said.

Private credit has expanded beyond its traditional focus on leveraged finance to include financing the real economy across infrastructure and asset-based credit. This diversification has opened up new opportunities for both borrowers and investors in a market that continues to evolve rapidly, he said.

Borrowers are increasingly drawn to private credit because of the “speed, certainty, flexibility, more customized solutions” it offers, according to Leiter.

For investors, the attraction is clear: “Investors are getting excess spread… in the context of 150 to 200 basis points of pickup, both in the high yield market or non-investment grade market, but even in the investment grade market,” he said, noting that for investment grade in particular, “that’s game changing.”

The insurance sector has become a significant player in the private credit market. “Insurance for us now is at $250B of capital with us, with Blackstone,” Leiter said, highlighting the growing institutional appetite for these investments.

He noted that insurance companies are increasingly allocating capital to private credit, with some U.S. and U.K. partners now having over 30% of their balance sheets in these investments.

Despite concerns about potential risks in this rapidly expanding market, Leiter counters that private credit may actually be safer than public credit.

“I think there’s a perception that private credit is inherently more risky than public credit. This is not the case,” he argued, pointing to performance metrics to support his view. “If you look at the performance in our actual portfolios, we’re running at half a percent default rate. The public leverage loan market is above 3% now.”

data.title: Sanofi making all insulin meds available for $35 a month in US
data.description:
• Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) is expanding a program that will allow all US patients the ability to purchase any of its insulin medications for $35 a month.
  • The company's Insulins Valyou Savings Program previously allowed $35 monthly insulin, but only for those without health insurance. With the change, anyone with a prescription, regardless of insurance status, can access the cheaper insulins.
  • The expanded eligibility goes into effect on Jan. 1, 2026
  • Sanofi's main insulin products include Lantus (insulin glargine), Toujeo (insulin glargine), Apidra (insulin glulisine), and Soliqua (insulin glargine and lixisenatide). 
  • Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO), both of whom have large diabetes franchises, have previously instituted $35 a month insulin programs. The Inflation Reduction Act also capped insulin expenses at that amount starting in 2023 for those on Medicare.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks initiated with bullish views at Tigress Financial
data.description:
Tigress Financial Partners began coverage of Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ:PANW) with a Buy rating and a 12-month target price of $245.

Shares of Palo Alto were largely flat but in the green on Friday.

Analyst Ivan Feinseth said Palo Alto's accelerating expansion in AI-powered security solutions,integrated platformization, and strong momentum in its subscription-based Next-Generation Security will continue to drive significant revenue growth, positioning it as a premium security play for long-term growth investors.

The company's fourth quarter results highlighted strong revenue growth, robust momentum in next-generation security, and continued strategic platform adoption, the analyst added.

"The combination of PANW’s integrated AI-Driven Platform, Cloud Security Expansion, and Strategic Acquisitions, margin expansion, and increasing market share gains is driving accelerating Business Performance trends," said Feinseth.

The analyst noted that the company is well-positioned to benefit from the accelerating global demand for cybersecurity, powered by sophisticated cyber threats, rapid digital transformation, cloud migration, generative AI disruptions, new regulatory mandates, and heightened security needs across sectors, including financial services, cybersecurity warfare, and the requirement for secure infrastructure.

"PANW’s disciplined capital allocation, driven by highimpact acquisitions, prioritization of investments in R&D, and efficient cash flow management, continues to drive ongoing growth and positions it for sustained leadership and accelerated growth," said Feinseth.

data.title: Consumer sentiment roughly stable with initial September reading; inflation expectations tick down
data.description:
U.S. Consumer Sentiment Index: 55.1 in September, revised from 55.4 in prior estimate, and 58.2 in August, according to data released by the University of Michigan on Friday.

One-year implied inflation: +4.7% vs. +4.8% prior estimate and +4.8% in August.

Five-year inflation expectations: +3.7% vs. prior September reading of +3.9% and +3.5% in August.

Current conditions index: 60.4 vs. previous estimate of 61.2 and 61.7 in prior month.

Consumer expectations: 51.7 vs. 51.8 prior estimate and 55.9 in August.

Developing... check back for updates.

data.title: Crypto exchange Kraken closes $500M fundraise, valuing it at $15B - report
data.description:
Kraken has closed a $500M funding round, valuing the crypto exchange at $15B in the run-up to an expected initial public offering, according to a recent media report.

The fundraise, which closed this month, did not feature a primary investor, Fortune reported, citing a person familiar with the matter. The terms, including the valuation, were set by Kraken.

Kraken co-CEO Arjun Sethi, along with investment managers and venture capitalists, were among the backers in the round, the person added.

Until this year, Kraken was said to have raised only $27M in venture capital funding, the article said, noting the crypto platform raked in nearly $80M in post-EBITDA earnings in Q2.

Like many of its rivals, the company has made a number of pro-growth moves this year, from launching crypto derivatives trading in the U.S. to acquiring Israel-based automation company Capitalise.ai, amid a friendlier regulatory environment under the Trump administration.

Kraken declined to comment to Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Brandywine Realty Trust cuts dividend by almost half, estimates Q4 earnings charge
data.description:
• Brandywine Realty Trust (NYSE:BDN) declared $0.08/share quarterly dividend, -46.7% decrease from prior dividend of $0.15.
  • Forward yield 7.24%
  • Payable Oct. 23; for shareholders of record Oct. 9; ex-div Oct. 9.
  • "During October, we intend to prepay a secured loan using a combination of cash on hand, our $600 million unsecured line-of-credit and proceeds from additional sources of liquidity, as appropriate," said the REIT in a September 26 statement.
  • "The secured loan totals $245 million and has a scheduled maturity date in February 2028 and is currently secured by 7 properties totaling 1.4 million square feet located in several of our segments. If the secured loan is prepaid, Brandywine will have a wholly owned operating portfolio that is fully unencumbered," added the statement.
  • "We estimate the prepayment will result in a fourth quarter earnings charge totaling $12-$14 million, or $0.07-$0.09 per common share which is currently not included in our 2025 earnings guidance," said the statement.
  • Shares were -1.56% during Friday morning trade to $4.42.
  • See BDN Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: First Merchants downgraded at Raymond James post First Savings acquisition announcement
data.description:
First Merchants (NASDAQ:FRME) was downgraded at Raymond James & Associates following the financial holding company's announcement of First Savings Financial Group (NASDAQ:FSFG) acquisition.

First Merchants said yesterday that it has entered into a definitive merger agreement to acquire FSFG in an all-stock transaction valued at ~$241.3M.

"Though the deal creates entry into the Louisville MSA, boosts EPS forecasts, and was executed at a fair market price (1.35x P/TBV), the use of capital lessens the likelihood of share repurchases and material securities restructurings near-term, while its core NIM path faces some headwinds with lower rates," said analyst Daniel Tamayo.

"Finally, the acquired specialty business lines from First Savings offer no/minimal funding sources and could create incremental funding headwinds at a time when organic lending opportunities appear strong, driving our belief the deal could delay its path towards core financial improvement," said the analyst in a research note.

"With several quarters necessary to prove out the merits of the deal along with NIM headwinds from lower rates despite forecasts for solid profitability, growth and credit metrics to continue, we see fewer near-term catalysts for shares to outperform, trading mostly in-line with peers," said the note.

Raymond James cut its recommendation on FRME to Market Perform from Outperform, and removed the $45.00 price target on the stock.

The rating on the stock aligns with Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating of Hold. Meanwhile, SA authors as well as the sell-side analysts grade the stock as Buy.

data.title: Barkin sees 'shaky' ground on Fed's dual mandate, but path ahead looks 'stable'
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond President Tom Barkin said Friday "the ground may look shaky" for the U.S. central bank, with inflation still above target and downward pressure on employment, though the path ahead looks "stable" if the Fed stays focused on balance.

"Since 2022, with inflation above our target and the labor market healthy, it made sense for us to focus our attention on inflation," Barkin said in a prepared speech at the Peterson Institute for International Economics.

"Now, with inflation risks still elevated but the downside risks to employment rising, we face a more challenging situation," he said. "We are at the top of the telephone pole, if you will. With our two mandate goals in tension, our focus is more about balance."

When looking further ahead, though, there are grounds for optimism on both inflation and employment.

On the jobs side, he noted that employment growth has retreated markedly, while the unemployment rate remained relatively stable. "There may be fewer jobs to fill, but there are also fewer people vying for each job," he said.

Barkin added that labor "demand remains solid and could even get a boost from stimulus embedded in the recent tax bill."

He contended that upside risks to inflation should also be limited, with businesses facing consumer pushback to passing on higher import costs from tariffs.

The 25-basis-point rate cut at the Federal Open Market Committee meeting last week "should help support the labor market while maintaining pressure on inflation, in the interest of keeping the economy on path to that more stable horizon," the Richmond Fed chief said.
